summary: Gruber surveys every available Twitter client for the iPhone in 2008 and finds none satisfactory. The core problem is Twitter's API, which has disabled paging beyond the 20 most recent tweets for over six months, crippling all third-party clients. Twitter.com itself renders correctly on Mobile Safari but is unoptimized—tiny tap targets, broken posting UI, and sluggish JavaScript. The mobile interface (m.twitter.com) is fast but feature-poor, and Twitter's switch to user-agent sniffing from separate URLs made it worse. Third-party clients like Hahlo, iTweet, PocketTweets, and Thincloud each have strengths but all share the paging limitation and various UI shortcomings. Gruber concludes that Twitter should be the ideal iPhone web app but isn't, because no single client handles both reading and posting well.
key_points: Twitter's API has disabled paging for over six months, limiting all third-party clients to the 20 most recent tweets—a deal-breaking limitation that Twitter's own website doesn't share.
Twitter.com's UI controls are absurdly small for touch interaction: 18x18px buttons are just 16% the size of Apple's recommended 44x44px minimum, and they're rendered even smaller when scaled.
Twitter's switch from URL-based mobile/desktop separation to cookie-based user-agent sniffing removed the ability to bookmark and tab between both interfaces—a clear regression.
JavaScript-powered live character counts cause typing lag on every third-party client, revealing a fundamental tension between rich UI features and Mobile Safari's performance limitations.
No single client handles both reading and posting well, forcing users into an unsatisfying split between clients for different tasks.
money_quotes: Twitter and the iPhone seem, at a glance, a perfect match: bite-sized micro-content paired with the world's best mobile web reader. But here's the thing: there's not yet a single good iPhone Twitter client.
In terms of area, an 18 x 18 px button is just 16 percent the size of a 44 x 44 px button.
If ever there was a web app that could be — should be — better on the iPhone than on a desktop browser, Twitter is it. But it isn't.
Using one Twitter client for reading and another for posting is like getting your sandwich at Burger King and your fries from McDonald's — convenience is the whole point.
You shouldn't have to press a button to figure out what it does.
Editing is where Hahlo is a Viking.
For posting, the Twitter.com interface is a disaster on the iPhone.
It seems weird that sites like Facebook and Amazon, which do so much more than Twitter, have iPhone-optimized interfaces, but Twitter does not.
key_insight: Twitter's failure to provide a good iPhone experience—through both its own unoptimized website and a crippled API that hamstrings third-party developers—represents a missed opportunity for what should be the ideal mobile web application.
